h1: Stuck at Customs? The New BIS Exemption Rule Can Save Your Import Shipment

blocks.text: Stuck at Customs? The New BIS Exemption Rule Can Save Your Import Shipment
DPIIT's new Feb 2026 notification offers a 180-day BIS exemption for transitional imports. Learn how to clear your customs shipment legally with BookMyTM.
Written By
BookMyTM BIS Consultant
Published
March 14, 2026

Imagine this nightmare scenario: You are the procurement head of a mid-sized manufacturing firm in India. Six months ago, you finalized a massive Purchase Order (PO) for ₹50 Lakhs worth of specialized electrical components or industrial raw materials from a trusted supplier in Germany or China. You opened the Letter of Credit, the factory produced the goods, and the cargo was loaded onto a ship bound for an Indian port.

While your container is navigating the ocean, the Indian Government suddenly issues a Quality Control Order (QCO), making Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) certification legally mandatory for that specific product category.

Fast forward to today. Your cargo arrives at Nhava Sheva or Chennai port. You file the Bill of Entry, expecting a routine clearance. Instead, the Customs Officer red-flags your shipment. Because the goods do not bear the mandatory ISI mark, they are deemed illegal for import. Your container is detained. Every single day it sits at the port, crushing demurrage (warehouse) charges pile up. You cannot sell the goods, you cannot use them in your factory, and returning them to the foreign supplier will cost you a fortune in reverse logistics.
For years, this exact "transitional" nightmare has paralyzed Indian importers, trapped millions of dollars in working capital, and disrupted critical domestic supply chains.

However, on February 12, 2026, the Department for Promotion of Industry and Internal Trade (DPIIT) intervened. Through a landmark official notification, the government has finally provided a legal escape route for goods caught in this exact crossfire. If your shipment is stuck at customs—or is currently on the water—this new exemption rule is your ultimate lifeline.

Here is a complete breakdown of the DPIIT's 2026 transitional import relief, the severe financial impacts of mishandling your customs paperwork, and exactly how to secure your clearance before port fees destroy your profit margins.
The Update: Decoding DPIIT Notification S.O. 776(E) (Simplified)

The government is aggressively expanding the mandatory BIS certification regime across hundreds of product categories to curb the influx of sub-standard foreign goods. While the intent is excellent for domestic quality, the abrupt enforcement dates often clash with the reality of global shipping timelines, which can take months from order placement to final delivery.
Recognizing this logistical impossibility, the DPIIT issued Notification S.O. 776(E) in February 2026 to introduce the "Transitional Import Exemption."

This rule effectively states that if an importer ordered goods before a new QCO became legally enforceable, those specific goods are exempt from immediate BIS certification requirements, allowing them to clear Indian customs seamlessly.
The Strict Conditions for the 180-Day Window:
The government is not handing out a blanket free pass. To prevent importers from exploiting the rule by backdating fake invoices, the exemption is heavily conditional and heavily audited:

items: The Pre-Dating Rule: You must prove with absolute certainty that the official Purchase Order (PO) and the commercial Letter of Credit (LC) were generated and legally executed before the date the relevant QCO was published in the Official Gazette.

The 180-Day Transit Limit: The exemption is only valid for consignments where the Bill of Lading (proof of shipping) proves that the goods were dispatched and arrived at the Indian port within a maximum of 180 days from the QCO enforcement date.

The Post-Clearance Reporting: The exemption does not mean you disappear off the radar. Once customs releases your goods, you have a strictly enforced 7-day window to upload all exemption documents, Bills of Entry, and usage declarations to the centralized BIS portal.

blocks.text: The Impact: Demurrage, Confiscation, and Supply Chain Collapse

Understanding the exemption is one thing; successfully executing it at a chaotic customs terminal is entirely different. If your customs broker or internal finance team is unaware of Notification S.O. 776(E), or if they submit the paperwork incorrectly, the financial fallout is rapid and devastating.
1. The Silent Killer: Demurrage and Detention Charges

When Customs detains your container for lacking an ISI mark, the port authorities grant a very short "free period" (usually 3 to 5 days). After that, demurrage charges begin. These are penalty fees for occupying port space, and they compound aggressively. A ₹50 Lakh shipment can easily accumulate ₹2 Lakhs in demurrage fees in just a few weeks of administrative delays. If you spend a month arguing with customs without utilizing the S.O. 776(E) exemption protocol, your profit margin on those goods will be completely wiped out by port fees alone.
2. Absolute Confiscation and Forced Re-Export

If you cannot prove your eligibility for the transitional exemption, the Customs Department has zero flexibility. Under the BIS Act and Customs regulations, importing non-compliant QCO goods is a strict liability offense. The adjudicating officer will issue a formal seizure memo. You will be given two terrible choices: either pay a massive penalty and legally "re-export" the goods back to the country of origin at your own expense, or abandon the cargo entirely, allowing the government to destroy it. In both scenarios, your investment goes to zero.
3. Factory Downtime and Breach of Contract

If you are importing raw materials or specialized machinery components, a customs detention doesn't just hurt your bank account; it stops your factory floor. Missing your domestic delivery targets because your raw materials are stuck at Nhava Sheva leads to breached B2B contracts, lost clients, and severe reputational damage in the market.
The Solution: BookMyTM’s Customs Exemption Protocol

At BookMyTM, we understand that when your cargo is detained, every single hour costs you money. You cannot afford to engage in a learning curve with the Customs Department. You need a specialized legal and compliance team that knows exactly how to trigger the DPIIT exemption instantly.
Our dedicated BIS & Import Compliance division manages the entire transitional relief process from end to end, ensuring your goods move from the port to your warehouse without friction.

items: 1. Immediate Exemption Eligibility Audit: The moment you notify us of a pending import or a customs red flag, our compliance strategists conduct a rapid audit. We cross-reference the specific HSN code of your goods against the latest 2026 BIS QCO master lists. We analyze the exact timestamps on your Purchase Orders, Proforma Invoices, and Letters of Credit to legally establish your right to the exemption under S.O. 776(E).

2. Drafting the Legal Declarations: Customs officers require highly specific, notarized declarations confirming that the goods were transitional and will be used exactly as prescribed by the law. Our legal team drafts these airtight affidavits and indemnities, ensuring there is no ambiguity that could give an assessing officer a reason to reject your claim.

3. Direct BIS Portal Management: The relief scheme requires mandatory post-clearance compliance. We take the administrative burden off your shoulders. Within the strict 7-day post-clearance window, our team compiles your Customs Out-of-Charge orders, Bills of Entry, and utilization reports, uploading them directly to the Bureau of Indian Standards portal to close the compliance loop and prevent future show-cause notices.

4. Long-Term FMCS Strategy: The 180-day exemption is a temporary band-aid, not a permanent cure. If you intend to continue importing this product, your foreign supplier must obtain a BIS license. While we rescue your current shipment, we simultaneously initiate the Foreign Manufacturers Certification Scheme (FMCS) process for your overseas factory, ensuring your future imports never face a customs blockade again.

blocks.text: "Global trade is complicated enough without your cargo being held hostage by sudden regulatory shifts. The government has provided a legal pathway to clear your transitional goods—make sure you have the right experts to navigate it."

Actionable Advice: 3 Steps to Secure Your Cargo Today

Do not wait until the Customs Officer sends you a detention memo. Proactive supply chain management is the only way to survive the tightening BIS regulatory environment in 2026. Take these three steps immediately:
Audit Pending POs

Pull a report of every international PO not yet in India. Check the product categories against the newest 2026 BIS QCO master lists. Flag affected shipments for the S.O. 776(E) exemption protocol immediately.
Secure Date-Stamped Docs

Ensure your foreign supplier has clearly mentioned the exact date of the Purchase Order on every shipping document (Commercial Invoice, Packing List, Bill of Lading). Any discrepancy will result in rejection.
Prepare Files Early

Do not wait for the vessel to arrive. Have your legal affidavits, PO proofs, and exemption letters fully drafted, signed, and handed to your Customs House Agent (CHA) while the ship is still in transit.
